raw_consumer_statement: Citibank denied my dispute for {$660.00} claiming insufficient evidence despite video proof that Best Buy has possession of the defective merchandise. On XX/XX/year> I bought a dryer advertised as excellent that doesnt work. On XX/XX/year> I brought it to Best Buy for return after several online return failed due to Best Buy return process not working. Manager admitted product was defective and inspection wasnt done. Best Buy took possession inside store. When my sister documented this, manager called police and had employees stage the dryer outside to make it look abandoned. After police left, Best Buy took dryer back inside - I have VIDEO of this. Best Buy told police I abandoned it ( LIE - proven by video ). I filed police follow-up report with evidence. Best Buy told Citibank they dont have the dryer ( LIE - proven by video ). Citibank sided with Best Buy without reviewing my video evidence. Im being billed for merchandise I DONT HAVE that the MERCHANT POSSESSES ( proven on video ). This violates Fair Credit Billing Act - consumers cant pay for goods in merchants possession. I have : video of Best Buy taking dryer, 2 police reports, receipts, professional inspection proving defect, witness testimony.
